title: Long-term diversifying afforestation enhances soil microbial network complexity and stability.
author_string: Yu, Haoran, Ochoa-Hueso, Raúl, Wang, Yuzhi, Liu, Chenming, Hu, Yongning et al.
abstract: Afforestation is one of the most effective techniques to regenerate degraded soils and alleviate climate change. Tree plantations have been shown to induce changes in soil microbial composition and diversity, but the effects of increasing tree diversity on soil microbial networks remain understudied, and there is less information about how the effects of afforestation on soil networks develop across soil depths (i.e., shallow vs. deep soil layers). Here, we investigated the long-term influence (>45 years) of tree planting at three diversity levels: from low (1 species), to medium (3 species), and high (6 species) number of tree species on the microbial networks of surface (0-10 cm) and deep (50-60 cm) soils across the Inner Mongolia Plateau. Afforestation with greater number of species (3 and 6) enhanced bacterial and fungal diversity and the stability and complexity of bacterial and fungal networks in both soil layers. Structural equation modeling revealed that the positive impact of tree diversity on microbial network complexity was driven by greater soil total carbon content and alpha diversity of microorganisms. In summary, diversifying afforestation may be a highly useful strategy for enhancing belowground microbial diversity and their interactions during ecosystem restoration, with potential knock-on consequences for the delivery of ecosystem services provided by soils.
